words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God, to the saints who are [in Ephesus] and faithful in Christ Jesus:
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, Strong's G652) denotes one sent with delegated authority, not merely a messenger. The phrase "en Ephesō" appears in brackets because several early manuscripts (P46, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) omit the location, suggesting this may have been a circular letter. HISTORICAL: This has led many scholars to view Ephesians as an encyclical epistle intended for multiple churches in the province of Asia.

verses.flow: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the one having blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Eulogētos" (εὐλογητός, G2128) is a verbal adjective meaning "worthy of praise/blessing," used exclusively of God in the NT. The threefold occurrence of the eulog- root (eulogētos, eulogēsas, eulogia) creates a powerful wordplay: "Blessed be the one who blessed us with every blessing." STRUCTURE: Verses 3-14 form one continuous sentence in the Greek, the longest in the NT, functioning as a grand berakah (Jewish blessing formula). TYPOLOGY: The phrase "en tois epouraniois" (in the heavenly places) appears five times in Ephesians alone, establishing the cosmic scope of God's redemptive work.

verses.flow: even as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world, for us to be holy and blameless before Him in love,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Exelexato" (ἐξελέξατο, G1586) is aorist middle voice — He chose for Himself — emphasizing divine initiative before creation. "Pro katabolēs kosmou" (before the foundation of the world) places election in eternity past, prior to any human action. GREEK: "Katabolē" (G2602) literally means "a casting down" or "a laying of a foundation," used of the founding act of creation itself (Heb 4:3, Matt 13:35). "Amōmos" (G299, blameless/without blemish) is sacrificial language drawn from the LXX requirement for unblemished offerings (Lev 1:3, 3:1).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: having predetermined us unto adoption as sons through Jesus Christ unto Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will,
verses.note: GREEK: "Proorisas" (προορίσας, G4309) means "having marked out beforehand" or "having set boundaries in advance," from pro (before) + horizō (to determine/set a boundary). "Huiothesia" (υἱοθεσία, G5206) is a legal term from Roman adoption law by which the adopted son received the full rights and inheritance of a natural-born son. HISTORICAL: Under Roman law, adoption was irrevocable and the adopted son's former debts were cancelled — a powerful metaphor for the believer's new legal standing.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: unto the praise of the glory of His grace, with which He graced us in the Beloved,
verses.note: GREEK: "Echaritōsen" (ἐχαρίτωσεν, G5487) is a rare verb appearing only here and in Luke 1:28 (to Mary). It means "to bestow grace upon" or "to endue with grace" — literally "He graced us." TYPOLOGY: "The Beloved" (ho ēgapēmenos) echoes the Father's declaration at Christ's baptism (Matt 3:17) and transfiguration (Matt 17:5), and recalls Isaac as the beloved son of Abraham (Gen 22:2 LXX), the one through whom the inheritance comes.

verses.flow: in whom we have the redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, according to the riches of His grace,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apolytrōsis" (ἀπολύτρωσις, G629) denotes a release effected by the payment of a ransom price, drawn from the slave-market. "Paraptōma" (G3900, trespass) literally means "a falling beside" or "a stepping aside," implying deviation from the path of righteousness. THEOLOGICAL: Redemption is explicitly grounded in Christ's blood — the cross as the ransom price — not in moral improvement or ritual observance. This echoes the Passover lamb typology (Exod 12:13) and the Day of Atonement blood rites (Lev 16:15-16).

verses.flow: which He caused to abound unto us in all wisdom and understanding,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: having made known to us the mystery of His will, according to His good pleasure which He purposed in Himself,

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: unto a stewardship of the fullness of the times, to sum up all things in Christ, the things upon the heavens and the things upon the earth, in Him,
verses.note: GREEK: "Anakephalaiōsasthai" (ἀνακεφαλαιώσασθαι, G346) is a remarkable compound verb meaning "to sum up under one head" or "to recapitulate all things under a single heading." It appears in the NT only here and in Rom 13:9. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The scope of this reunification explicitly includes "the things upon the heavens" — the heavenly realm of spiritual powers — and "the things upon the earth," indicating Christ's kingship restores order to both the divine council realm and the human realm, reversing the fracturing described in the Deuteronomy 32 worldview (Deut 32:8-9 LXX). THEOLOGICAL: "Oikonomia" (G3622) denotes a household administration or stewardship, here referring to God's cosmic plan executed at the appointed time.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: in whom also we were made an inheritance, having been predetermined according to the purpose of the one working all things according to the counsel of His will,

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: unto our being for the praise of His glory, we who had beforehand hoped in Christ,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: in whom you also, having heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation, in whom also having believed, you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise,
verses.note: GREEK: "Esphragisthēte" (ἐσφραγίσθητε, G4972) means "you were sealed," an aorist passive indicative. A seal (sphragis) in the ancient world marked ownership, authenticity, and protection. THEOLOGICAL: The Holy Spirit Himself functions as the seal — not a separate act from the Spirit, but the Spirit's indwelling presence is the seal. "The Spirit of promise" (to pneuma tēs epangelias) is the Spirit who was promised in the OT prophets (Joel 2:28-29; Ezek 36:26-27; Isa 44:3), marking the new covenant age.

verses.flow: who is the earnest of our inheritance, unto the redemption of the possession, unto the praise of His glory.
verses.note: GREEK: "Arrabōn" (ἀρραβών, G728) is a Semitic loanword (Hebrew 'erabon, Gen 38:17-18) denoting a down payment or pledge guaranteeing the full amount to follow. THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit is not merely a symbol of future inheritance but a present installment of the glory to come — the same substance, given in part now, to be received in full at the "redemption of the possession" (apolytrōsis tēs peripoiēseōs), referring to the final bodily resurrection and cosmic restoration (Rom 8:23).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: On account of this, I also, having heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus and your love toward all the saints,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: do not cease giving thanks on your behalf, making mention in my prayers,
that the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, might give to you a spirit of wisdom and of revelation in the full knowledge of Him,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The God of our Lord Jesus Christ" preserves the full divine-human reality of the incarnation — even the exalted Christ relates to the Father as His God (John 20:17). "The Father of glory" echoes the Shekinah tradition: God is the source and possessor of all divine radiance. GREEK: "Epignōsis" (ἐπίγνωσις, G1922) is an intensified form of gnōsis (knowledge), denoting full, experiential, relational knowledge rather than mere intellectual apprehension. "Apokalypsis" (G602) means an unveiling — spiritual sight is a gift, not a human achievement.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: the eyes of your heart having been enlightened, that you may know what is the hope of His calling, what the riches of the glory of His inheritance in the saints,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: and what is the surpassing greatness of His power toward us who believe, according to the working of the might of His strength,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: which He worked in Christ, having raised Him from the dead and seated Him at His right hand in the heavenly places,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The resurrection and enthronement of Christ at God's right hand fulfills Psalm 110:1 (LXX 109:1), the most quoted OT passage in the NT: "Sit at my right hand." Christ's exaltation follows the pattern of the Davidic king's enthronement but surpasses it infinitely in scope. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The seating "in the heavenly places" (en tois epouraniois) places Christ on the divine council throne itself, at the right hand of the Most High — the position of supreme executive authority over all spiritual beings.

words.m: verb present passive participle genitive singular neuter
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: far above every rule and authority and power and dominion and every name being named, not only in this age but also in the one about to come,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The four terms — "archē" (rule/principality, G746), "exousia" (authority, G1849), "dynamis" (power, G1411), and "kyriotēs" (dominion/lordship, G2963) — represent classes of spiritual beings in the heavenly hierarchy. Christ is enthroned "hyperanō" (far above) every rank of celestial power, both in the present age and the age to come. THEOLOGICAL: "Every name being named" encompasses all spiritual authorities, whether loyal to God or rebellious — none stands outside Christ's supreme sovereignty (Phil 2:9-11).

verses.flow: and He subjected all things under His feet, and gave Him as head over all things to the assembly,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "He subjected all things under His feet" directly quotes Psalm 8:6 (LXX), which describes the dominion originally given to Adam. Christ as the last Adam (1 Cor 15:27, 45) fulfills the mandate of Genesis 1:26-28 that the first Adam forfeited. GREEK: "Kephalē" (κεφαλή, G2776) meaning "head" carries the dual sense of authority/rule and source/origin. Christ is given as head "hyper panta" (over all things) to the assembly — His headship over creation is exercised for the benefit of His people.

words.g: which is put in to fill up

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: which is His body, the fullness of the one filling all things in all.
verses.note: GREEK: "Plērōma" (πλήρωμα, G4138) means "fullness" or "that which fills up." The church as Christ's body is the fullness of Christ, who Himself fills all things in all. THEOLOGICAL: The closing phrase "tou ta panta en pasin plēroumenou" can be read as middle voice ("the one filling all in all") rather than passive ("being filled"), affirming Christ's active, sovereign filling of all creation with His presence and rule. The church is thus the visible, corporate manifestation of Christ's cosmic lordship — His body through which He exercises His reign in the present age.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And you being dead in the trespasses and the sins of you,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: in which you once walked according to the age of this world, according to the ruler of the authority of the air, of the spirit now working in the sons of disobedience,
verses.note: GREEK: "αἰῶνα τοῦ κόσμου τούτου" (aiōna tou kosmou toutou) literally "the age of this world"—a double expression combining temporal (age/era) and spatial (world-system) dimensions of the present evil order. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "τὸν ἄρχοντα τῆς ἐξουσίας τοῦ ἀέρος" (ton archonta tēs exousias tou aeros) — "the ruler of the authority of the air" identifies Satan as a cosmic potentate governing the spiritual domain between heaven and earth. In Second Temple Jewish cosmology, the "air" was understood as the realm inhabited by hostile spiritual powers (cf. Testament of Benjamin 3:4; Eph 6:12). The genitive chain identifies this ruler as operating through a spirit of disobedience that energizes (ἐνεργοῦντος, energountos) rebellious humanity.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: among whom also we all once conducted ourselves in the desires of our flesh, doing the wishes of the flesh and of the thoughts, and we were by nature children of wrath, even as the rest—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul shifts from "you" (Gentiles) to "we all" (including Jews), establishing universal condemnation under sin. "τέκνα φύσει ὀργῆς" (tekna physei orgēs) — "children by nature of wrath" — indicates that the condition of being under divine wrath is not merely behavioral but inherent to fallen human nature. This is a foundational text for the doctrine of original sin. GREEK: "ἀνεστράφημεν" (anestraphēmen, Strong's G390) means "conducted ourselves" or "lived among," carrying a sense of habitual manner of life.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: but God, being rich in mercy, on account of His great love with which He loved us,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: even us being dead in the trespasses, He made alive together with Christ—by grace you are having been saved—
verses.note: GREEK: "συνεζωοποίησεν" (synezōopoiēsen, Strong's G4806) — "made alive together with" — is a compound verb with the prefix σύν (syn, "together with"), emphasizing the believer's union with Christ in His resurrection life. The parenthetical "χάριτί ἐστε σεσῳσμένοι" (chariti este sesōsmenoi) uses a periphrastic perfect: "by grace you are having been saved" — the perfect tense indicating a completed past action with continuing present results. TYPOLOGY: As God breathed life into Adam (Gen 2:7), so He makes dead sinners alive in the second Adam, Christ (1 Cor 15:22, 45).

verses.flow: and He raised us up together and seated us together in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Three σύν- (syn-) compound verbs span vv.5-6: made alive together with, raised together, seated together — each binding believers to Christ's resurrection, ascension, and heavenly enthronement. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις" (en tois epouraniois) — "in the heavenly places" — the same realm where spiritual powers operate (Eph 1:20-21; 3:10; 6:12). Believers are positionally enthroned with Christ above the hostile spiritual authorities mentioned in v.2.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: in order that He might show forth in the ages coming the surpassing riches of His grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For by grace you are having been saved through faith; and this not of yourselves, it is the gift of God—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the locus classicus for the Protestant doctrine of sola gratia and sola fide. Grace (χάρις, charis) is the instrumental cause; faith (πίστις, pistis) is the means. "Τοῦτο" (touto, neuter "this") does not agree grammatically with either "grace" (feminine) or "faith" (feminine), indicating that the entire arrangement of salvation-by-grace-through-faith is "the gift of God." GREEK: The periphrastic perfect "ἐστε σεσῳσμένοι" (este sesōsmenoi) emphasizes the abiding state of having been saved — completed yet enduring.

verses.flow: not of works, lest anyone should boast.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we are His workmanship, having been created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.
verses.note: GREEK: "ποίημα" (poiēma, Strong's G4161) — "workmanship" or "thing made" — from ποιέω (poieō, "to make"). English "poem" derives from this word. It emphasizes that believers are God's crafted work of art, not self-made. THEOLOGICAL: The verse resolves the tension between vv.8-9 and the role of works: good works are not the ground of salvation but its designed purpose. "προητοίμασεν" (proētoimasen) — "prepared beforehand" — indicates divine foreordination of the believer's path of obedience (cf. Eph 1:4-5, 11).

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: Therefore remember that once you, the nations in the flesh, the ones being called "uncircumcision" by the one being called "circumcision" made by hands in the flesh—

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: that you were at that time apart from Christ, having been alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of the promise, having no hope and without God in the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Five dimensions of Gentile alienation are enumerated: (1) apart from Christ, (2) alienated from Israel's commonwealth, (3) strangers to the covenants, (4) without hope, (5) without God (ἄθεοι, atheoi — the only NT occurrence of this word). HISTORICAL: "πολιτείας τοῦ Ἰσραήλ" (politeias tou Israēl) — "commonwealth of Israel" — uses a term from Greco-Roman civic life, conveying that Israel was a divinely constituted polity with covenant membership, from which Gentiles were excluded.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For He Himself is our peace, the One having made the both one and having broken down the middle wall of the partition, the enmity, in His flesh,
verses.note: GREEK: "τὸ μεσότοιχον τοῦ φραγμοῦ" (to mesotoichon tou phragmou) — "the middle wall of the partition" — μεσότοιχον (Strong's G3320) is a hapax legomenon in the NT. HISTORICAL: This likely alludes to the soreg, the stone barrier in the Jerusalem temple beyond which no Gentile could pass on pain of death. Inscriptions warning Gentiles have been archaeologically recovered. TYPOLOGY: Christ's flesh, torn on the cross, corresponds to the temple veil torn at His death (Matt 27:51), removing the barrier between God and humanity and between Jew and Gentile.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: having rendered inoperative the law of the commandments in ordinances, that the two He might create in Himself into one new man, making peace,
verses.note: GREEK: "καταργήσας" (katargēsas) — "having rendered inoperative" or "having abolished" — does not mean the moral law ceases to exist but that the law "of commandments in ordinances" (τὸν νόμον τῶν ἐντολῶν ἐν δόγμασιν) as a dividing barrier between Jew and Gentile has been nullified. THEOLOGICAL: The "one new man" (ἕνα καινὸν ἄνθρωπον) is neither Jew nor Gentile but a new humanity constituted in Christ — the new Adam and head of a new race (cf. 2 Cor 5:17; Gal 3:28).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and might reconcile them both in one body to God through the cross, having put to death the enmity by it.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come He proclaimed peace to you, the ones far off, and peace to the ones near.
For through Him we have access, the both of us, in one Spirit to the Father.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is profoundly Trinitarian in structure: access is through the Son (δι' αὐτοῦ), in one Spirit (ἐν ἑνὶ πνεύματι), to the Father (πρὸς τὸν πατέρα). GREEK: "προσαγωγήν" (prosagōgēn, Strong's G4318) — "access" or "introduction" — a term used for formal introduction into the presence of a king. Both groups ("οἱ ἀμφότεροι," hoi amphoteroi, "the both") share equal access to the divine throne.

verses.flow: So then you are no longer strangers and sojourners, but you are fellow-citizens of the saints and members of the household of God,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: having been built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus Himself being the chief cornerstone,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The temple imagery here echoes the Solomonic temple built upon a massive cornerstone (1 Kgs 5-6; Isa 28:16; Ps 118:22). Christ is the ἀκρογωνιαῖος (akrogōniaios) — the "chief cornerstone" (Strong's G204) — the stone that determines the alignment of the entire structure. THEOLOGICAL: The "foundation of the apostles and prophets" likely refers to New Testament apostles and prophets (cf. Eph 3:5; 4:11), as the foundation they laid through their testimony concerning Christ.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: in whom every building being fitted together grows into a holy temple in the Lord,
in whom you also are being built together into a dwelling place of God in the Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: "συνοικοδομεῖσθε" (synoikodomeisthe, Strong's G4925) — "are being built together" — present passive, indicating an ongoing divine construction project. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter concludes where it began — with the Gentile addressees ("you also") — but now they are no longer dead in sin (v.1) but being incorporated as living stones into God's dwelling place. "κατοικητήριον τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν πνεύματι" (katoikētērion tou theou en pneumati) — "a dwelling place of God in the Spirit" — the church as the new temple replaces the Jerusalem temple as the locus of God's presence among His people (cf. 1 Cor 3:16; 1 Pet 2:5).

verses.flow: For this cause I, Paul, the prisoner of Christ Jesus on behalf of you, the Gentiles—
verses.note: GREEK: "Désmios" (δέσμιος, Strong's G1198) means "prisoner" or "one bound in chains." Paul writes from imprisonment, likely in Rome (ca. AD 60-62). The sentence breaks off (anacolouthon) and resumes at v.14, indicating the parenthetical nature of vv.2-13. HISTORICAL: Paul's self-designation as "the prisoner of Christ Jesus" reframes Roman captivity as divine service—his chains belong to Christ, not Caesar.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: if indeed you have heard of the stewardship of the grace of God, the one having been given to me for you,
that according to revelation the mystery was made known to me, just as I wrote before in brief,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apokálypsis" (ἀποκάλυψις, G602) denotes an unveiling or disclosure of what was previously hidden. "Mystḗrion" (μυστήριον, G3466) is not a puzzle to be solved but a divine plan now revealed. THEOLOGICAL: Paul claims direct revelatory knowledge, not human tradition, as the source of his gospel—paralleling his testimony in Galatians 1:11-12.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: by reference to which you are able, reading, to perceive my understanding in the mystery of Christ,
which in other generations was not made known to the sons of men, as it has now been revealed to his holy apostles and prophets in the Spirit,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The mystery was not absolutely unknown in the OT but was not made known "as" (hōs) it has now been revealed—indicating a fuller, clearer disclosure in the apostolic era. The Spirit is the agent of this revelation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "sons of men" (tois huiois tōn anthrōpōn) contrasts the human realm with the divine council where God's purposes originate; what was veiled from the earthly realm is now openly declared.

verses.flow: that the Gentiles are fellow-heirs and of the same body and fellow-partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus through the gospel,
verses.note: GREEK: Three compound "syn-" (σύν) words appear here—"synklēronóma" (fellow-heirs), "sýssōma" (of the same body), and "symmétocha" (fellow-partakers)—all emphasizing the full incorporation of Gentiles. "Sýssōma" (G4954) is a Pauline hapax legomenon, likely coined by Paul to express the unprecedented union of Jew and Gentile in one body. TYPOLOGY: The "promise" (epangelía) traces back to the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 12:3; Gal 3:8, 14, 29), now fulfilled in Christ.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: of which I became a servant according to the gift of the grace of God, the one having been given to me according to the working of his power—
to me, the least of all the holy ones, was given this grace— to proclaim as good news to the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Elachistotérō" (ἐλαχιστοτέρῳ) is a double comparative—a comparative form built on an already superlative word (eláchistos, "least"), yielding something like "less than the least." This is grammatically irregular and likely intentional Pauline coinage expressing profound humility. "Anexichníastos" (ἀνεξιχνίαστον, G421) means "untraceable, unsearchable," used also of God's ways in Romans 11:33.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive singular neuter
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: and to bring to light for all what is the stewardship of the mystery having been hidden from the ages in God, the one having created all things,
in order that there might now be made known to the rulers and to the authorities in the heavenly places, through the assembly, the manifold wisdom of God,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Archaîs" (ἀρχαῖς, G746) and "exousíais" (ἐξουσίαις, G1849) refer to spiritual rulers and authorities in the heavenly places (epouránia)—members of the divine council and cosmic powers. The assembly (ekklēsía) becomes the instrument through which God displays his wisdom to these heavenly beings. THEOLOGICAL: "Polypoíkilos" (πολυποίκιλος, G4182) means "many-sided, exceedingly variegated"—a hapax legomenon in the NT. God's wisdom is not monochrome but multifaceted, demonstrated supremely in the union of Jew and Gentile.

verses.flow: according to the purpose of the ages which he accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord,
in whom we have boldness and access in confidence through faith in him,
Therefore I ask you not to lose heart at my afflictions on your behalf, which are your glory.
For this cause I bow my knees before the Father,
from whom every family in the heavens and upon earth is named,
verses.note: GREEK: "Patriá" (πατριά, G3965) means "family, lineage, clan" and derives from "patḗr" (father). Paul draws a deliberate wordplay: every "fathered-group" (patriá) derives its name and nature from the Father (patḗr). DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Every family in the heavens and upon earth" encompasses both angelic families (heavenly beings, divine council members) and human families—all deriving their identity from the one Father, affirming God's supreme headship over all realms of existence.

verses.flow: that he might grant you, according to the riches of his glory, to be strengthened with power through his Spirit in the inner man,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: that Christ might dwell through faith in your hearts, having been rooted and founded in love,
that you might have strength to comprehend, with all the holy ones, what is the breadth and length and height and depth,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: and to know the love of Christ which surpasses knowledge, that you might be filled unto all the fullness of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The love of Christ "surpassing knowledge" (hyperballousan tēs gnōseōs) indicates that experiential communion with Christ exceeds intellectual comprehension. "Plḗrōma toû theoû" (fullness of God) is among the most exalted phrases in Scripture—believers are to be filled unto the complete measure of divine fullness (cf. Col 1:19; 2:9-10). TYPOLOGY: The temple imagery of being "filled" echoes the glory of God filling the tabernacle (Exod 40:34) and temple (1 Kgs 8:11); the community of believers now becomes God's dwelling.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: Now to the one who is able to do far beyond all things, exceedingly above what we ask or think, according to the power working in us,
verses.note: GREEK: "Hyperekperissou" (ὑπερεκπερισσοῦ, G5228+G1537+G4053) is a triple-compounded adverb meaning "far beyond all measure, superabundantly beyond." This extreme intensification is characteristically Pauline (cf. 1 Thess 3:10; 5:13). STRUCTURE: Verses 20-21 form a doxology closing the first half of Ephesians, marking the transition from doctrinal exposition (chs. 1-3) to practical exhortation (chs. 4-6).

verses.flow: to him be the glory in the assembly and in Christ Jesus unto all the generations of the age of the ages. Amen.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Glory is ascribed both "in the assembly" and "in Christ Jesus"—the two are inseparable as the locus of God's self-glorification. "Unto all the generations of the age of the ages" (eis pásas tàs geneàs toû aiōnos tōn aiōnōn) is a uniquely expansive temporal expression, combining "generations" with the standard "age of ages" formula to convey unending, inexhaustible praise. STRUCTURE: This doxology forms the theological climax and structural hinge of the entire epistle.

verses.flow: I exhort you therefore, I the prisoner in the Lord, to walk worthily of the calling with which you were called,
verses.note: GREEK: "Parakalō" (Strong's G3870) carries the sense of both exhortation and comfort — here it functions as an apostolic appeal. "Desmios" (G1198), prisoner/one in bonds, reflects Paul's literal imprisonment, yet he qualifies it "en kyriō" (in the Lord), reframing his captivity as service to Christ rather than to Rome. HISTORICAL: Paul writes from prison, likely in Rome (c. AD 60–62), making this appeal carry the weight of one who embodies the suffering he calls others to endure.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: with all humility of mind and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: being diligent to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace.
verses.note: GREEK: "Spoudazontes" (G4704) implies urgent, zealous effort — not passive maintenance but active diligence. "Syndesmos" (G4886), bond or ligament, portrays peace as the binding sinew that holds unity intact. THEOLOGICAL: The unity is not one believers create but one the Spirit has already established; believers are called to preserve what God has accomplished.

verses.flow: One body and one Spirit, even as also you were called in one hope of your calling;
one Lord, one faith, one baptism,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sevenfold list of "ones" in vv.4–6 (one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father) forms a creedal confession structuring Trinitarian theology — the Spirit (v.4), the Lord Christ (v.5), and God the Father (v.6). This is among the earliest Trinitarian patterns in the New Testament.

verses.flow: one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all.
But to each one of us was given grace according to the measure of the gift of Christ.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore it says, "Having ascended on high, he led captivity captive, and gave gifts to men."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Psalm 68:18 (LXX 67:19), a victory psalm celebrating God's triumphal ascent of Mount Zion after conquering enemies. Paul applies this to Christ's ascension, recasting the divine warrior motif: Christ ascends to the heavenly throne and distributes gifts (the Spirit and gifted persons) to his people. The shift from "received gifts" (Hebrew/LXX) to "gave gifts" reflects an interpretive tradition also attested in the Targum on Psalms. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The imagery of ascending on high and leading captivity captive evokes the divine warrior's triumph over hostile spiritual powers (cf. Col 2:15).

verses.flow: Now this, "He ascended," what is it except that he also descended into the lower parts of the earth?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The lower parts of the earth" (ta katōtera merē tēs gēs) is debated — it may refer to the incarnation (descent to earth from heaven), to Hades/the realm of the dead, or to the grave. In light of the divine council framework, Christ's descent and ascent represent his complete sovereignty over every realm of the cosmos — heavenly, earthly, and subterranean (cf. Phil 2:9–10).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: He who descended, he himself is also the one who ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The one who descended is the very same one who ascended "hyperanō pantōn tōn ouranōn" — far above all the heavens. "Plērōsē ta panta" (that he might fill all things) declares Christ's cosmic lordship. He pervades and governs every sphere of creation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Christ's ascent above all heavens places him above every rank of spiritual being in the heavenly hierarchy (cf. Eph 1:20–21).

verses.flow: And he himself gave some as apostles, and some as prophets, and some as evangelists, and some as shepherds and teachers,
verses.note: GREEK: "Euangelistas" (G2099) appears only three times in the NT (here, Acts 21:8, 2 Tim 4:5) — one who announces the good news. "Poimen" (G4166), shepherd/pastor, is used here uniquely in the NT as a church office title. The single article governing "tous de poimenas kai didaskalous" suggests shepherds and teachers form a single functional category. STRUCTURE: The fivefold gifting (apostles, prophets, evangelists, shepherds-teachers) serves the threefold purpose stated in v.12.

verses.flow: for the equipping of the saints, unto the work of ministry, unto the building up of the body of Christ,

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: until we all attain unto the unity of the faith and of the full knowledge of the Son of God, unto a mature man, unto the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Epignōsis" (G1922) denotes full, experiential knowledge — not merely intellectual assent but deep relational knowing of the Son of God. "Andra teleion" (mature man) uses "anēr" (adult male) rather than "anthrōpos," emphasizing full maturity. "Plērōma tou Christou" (fullness of Christ) echoes the cosmic fullness language of Eph 1:23. THEOLOGICAL: Corporate maturity, not merely individual growth, is in view — the entire body attaining to the full stature of Christ.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: that we should no longer be infants, being tossed by waves and carried about by every wind of doctrine, in the trickery of men, in craftiness unto the scheming of deceit,
verses.note: GREEK: "Klydōnizomenoi" (G2831) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning to be tossed by waves — vivid maritime imagery for doctrinal instability. "Kybeia" (G2940), also a NT hapax, literally means "dice-playing," hence trickery or sleight. "Methodeia" (G3180) appears only here and in Eph 6:11, denoting crafty scheming — in 6:11 it is attributed to the devil, suggesting that doctrinal deception has a demonic dimension.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: but speaking the truth in love, we may grow up into him in all things, who is the head — Christ,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: from whom all the body, being fitted together and held together through every joint of supply, according to the working in measure of each individual part, makes the growth of the body unto the building up of itself in love.
This therefore I say and testify in the Lord, that you no longer walk as also the Gentiles walk, in the futility of their mind,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: being darkened in their understanding, having been alienated from the life of God, on account of the ignorance that is in them, on account of the hardening of their heart,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pōrōsis tēs kardias" (hardening of the heart) echoes the OT motif of Israel's hardened heart (Isa 6:10; Mark 3:5). Here it is applied to Gentiles, whose spiritual condition apart from God mirrors rebellious Israel. The causal chain is significant: hardening leads to ignorance, ignorance leads to alienation from God's life, and alienation produces darkened understanding.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: who, having become past feeling, gave themselves over to sensuality, unto the working of all uncleanness in greediness.
But you did not so learn Christ,
if indeed you heard him and were taught in him, even as truth is in Jesus,

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: that you put off, according to the former manner of life, the old man, the one being corrupted according to the desires of deceit,
and be renewed in the spirit of your mind,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: and put on the new man, the one created according to God in righteousness and holiness of the truth.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "new man created according to God" (ton kainon anthrōpon ton kata theon ktisthenta) echoes Genesis 1:26–27 — humanity made in God's image. The putting on of the new man is a new-creation motif: what was lost in Adam is restored in Christ (cf. Col 3:10; 2 Cor 5:17). "Dikaiosynē kai hosiotēti tēs alētheias" (righteousness and holiness of the truth) describes the moral character of this new creation.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, having put away falsehood, let each one speak truth with his neighbor, for we are members of one another.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Speak truth each one with his neighbor" quotes Zechariah 8:16 (LXX), connecting the ethics of the new community to the prophetic vision of restored Israel. The rationale — "for we are members of one another" — grounds ethical behavior in the one-body theology of vv.4 and 16.

verses.flow: Be angry, and do not sin; let not the sun go down upon your wrath,
verses.note: GREEK: "Orgizesthe kai mē hamartanete" quotes Psalm 4:4 (LXX), a command acknowledging that anger itself is not necessarily sinful, but must be governed. "Parorgismos" (G3950) is a NT hapax legomenon denoting a provocation to wrath — distinct from "orgē" (settled anger). The sunset deadline reflects Jewish reckoning of the day ending at sundown, urging same-day reconciliation.

verses.flow: neither give place to the devil.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Let the one who steals steal no longer, but rather let him labor, working with his own hands that which is good, that he may have something to share with the one having need.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let no corrupt word proceed out of your mouth, but if any is good for building up according to the need, that it may give grace to those who hear.
And do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, in whom you were sealed unto the day of redemption.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "To pneuma to hagion tou theou" (the Holy Spirit of God) is given full titular weight. "Esphragisthēte" (you were sealed) points back to Eph 1:13 — the Spirit is God's ownership seal and guarantee. Grieving the Spirit is possible precisely because he is personal, not an impersonal force. "Hēmeran apolytrōseōs" (day of redemption) refers to the eschatological consummation — the final deliverance of body and soul at Christ's return.

verses.flow: Let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and blasphemy be taken away from you, with all malice.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And become kind toward one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as also God in Christ forgave you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Chrēstoi" (G5543), kind or good, puns on "Christos" in early Christian tradition — believers are to be "Christ-like" in kindness. "Eusplanchnoi" (G2155), tenderhearted, literally means "having good intestines," as the ancients located compassion in the bowels. THEOLOGICAL: The ground of forgiveness is not human merit but divine action: "kathōs kai ho theos en Christō echarisato hymin" — even as God in Christ forgave you. Grace received becomes grace extended.

verses.flow: Become therefore imitators of God, as beloved children,
verses.note: GREEK: μιμηταί (mimētai, Strong's G3402) means "imitators" — used by Paul also in 1 Cor 11:1 and 1 Thess 1:6. The term evokes active, deliberate copying of a model, not passive admiration. The grounding phrase "as beloved children" (ὡς τέκνα ἀγαπητά) establishes the familial identity that makes imitation possible: one imitates a Father to whom one truly belongs.

verses.flow: and walk in love, just as also Christ loved us and gave himself over on behalf of us, an offering and a sacrifice to God for a fragrance of sweet aroma.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The language of "offering and sacrifice" (προσφοράν καὶ θυσίαν) and "fragrance of sweet aroma" (ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας) deliberately echoes Levitical sacrifice language (Lev 1:9, 13, 17 LXX). Christ's self-giving fulfills and surpasses the entire sacrificial system. The phrase ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας appears repeatedly in the LXX for offerings acceptable to God, identifying Christ's death as the ultimate pleasing sacrifice.

verses.flow: But sexual immorality and all uncleanness or covetousness, let it not even be named among you, as is fitting for saints,
and filthiness and foolish talk or coarse jesting, which are not fitting, but rather thanksgiving.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For this you know, recognizing that every sexually immoral person or unclean person or covetous one, who is an idolater, has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "the kingdom of Christ and of God" (τῇ βασιλείᾳ τοῦ Χριστοῦ καὶ θεοῦ) is remarkable — the single article τῇ governing both Χριστοῦ and θεοῦ under Granville Sharp's rule may indicate a shared divine identity between Christ and God. The covetous person is identified as an εἰδωλολάτρης (idolater, G1496), equating the worship of material gain with the worship of false gods — a theme rooted in the first commandment (Exod 20:3).

verses.flow: Let no one deceive you with empty words, for on account of these things the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Sons of disobedience" (τοὺς υἱοὺς τῆς ἀπειθείας) is a Hebraic idiom identifying those characterized by and belonging to the realm of disobedience — those under the dominion of the hostile spiritual powers mentioned in Eph 2:2. The "wrath of God" coming upon them reflects the divine judgment that falls on both the rebellious human and spiritual realms aligned against God's rule.

verses.flow: Do not therefore become fellow-partakers with them.
For you were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord; as children of light walk,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast is not merely moral but ontological: "you were darkness" (ἦτε σκότος), not merely "in darkness." Believers have undergone a transfer of identity and realm — from the domain of darkness to "light in the Lord" (φῶς ἐν κυρίῳ). This echoes Col 1:13 and the cosmic conflict framework of Ephesians (Eph 6:12). The imperative "walk as children of light" flows from the indicative of new identity.

verses.flow: for the fruit of the light is in all goodness and righteousness and truth,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: discerning what is well-pleasing to the Lord,
and do not participate in the unfruitful works of darkness, but rather even expose them.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: For the things done in secret by them are shameful even to speak of.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: But all things being exposed by the light are made manifest,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for everything that is made manifest is light. Therefore it says: "Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ shall shine upon you."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quoted saying "Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ shall shine upon you" does not match any single OT text exactly. It likely draws on Isa 26:19 and Isa 60:1 LXX, combining resurrection imagery with the dawn of divine glory. The verb ἐπιφαύσει (G2017, "shall shine upon") is a hapax legomenon in the NT, possibly adapted from the LXX of Isa 60:1 (φωτίζου). Early church tradition may have used this as a baptismal hymn fragment.

verses.flow: Look therefore carefully how you walk, not as unwise but as wise,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: redeeming the time, because the days are evil.
On account of this do not become foolish, but understand what the will of the Lord is.
And do not be drunk with wine, in which is dissipation, but be filled in the Spirit,
verses.note: GREEK: μεθύσκεσθε (methyskesthe, G3182) is present passive imperative — "do not go on being made drunk." The contrast with πληροῦσθε ἐν πνεύματι (be filled in/by the Spirit) is striking: where wine leads to ἀσωτία (asōtia, G810, "dissipation/reckless abandon" — same word used of the prodigal son in Luke 15:13), the Spirit produces ordered worship described in vv. 19-21. HISTORICAL: Dionysiac worship involving wine-induced ecstasy was well known in Ephesus, making this contrast culturally pointed.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: speaking to one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: giving thanks always for all things in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ to the God and Father,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: submitting yourselves to one another in the fear of Christ.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This participle ὑποτασσόμενοι ("submitting yourselves") is grammatically dependent on the imperative πληροῦσθε ("be filled") in v. 18, making mutual submission a fruit of Spirit-filling rather than an independent command. It also serves as a transitional hinge verse introducing the household code of 5:22–6:9. The phrase "in the fear of Christ" (ἐν φόβῳ Χριστοῦ) places Christ in the position of reverent awe normally reserved for God alone.

verses.flow: Wives, to your own husbands, as to the Lord,
because a husband is head of the wife, as also Christ is head of the assembly, he himself being savior of the body.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: κεφαλή (kephalē, G2776, "head") is used in a dual analogy: husband-wife and Christ-assembly. Paul specifies that Christ's headship is exercised as σωτὴρ τοῦ σώματος ("savior of the body"), defining the nature of this headship as salvific self-sacrifice rather than mere authority. This redefines all human headship in light of Christ's pattern of costly, life-giving love.

verses.flow: But as the assembly submits itself to Christ, so also the wives to their husbands in everything.
Husbands, love your wives, just as also Christ loved the assembly and gave himself over on behalf of her,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of the water in the word,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The washing of the water in the word" (τῷ λουτρῷ τοῦ ὕδατος ἐν ῥήματι) draws on priestly purification rites (Exod 29:4; Lev 8:6) and Ezekiel's promise of cleansing water (Ezek 36:25). Christ acts as both the priest who purifies and the bridegroom who prepares his bride. The ῥῆμα (rhēma, G4487, "spoken word") likely refers to the gospel proclamation or baptismal confession accompanying the washing.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: that he might present to himself the assembly in glory, not having spot or wrinkle or any such thing, but that she might be holy and without blemish.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: So also husbands ought to love their own wives as their own bodies. The one loving his own wife loves himself.
For no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it, just as also Christ the assembly,
because we are members of his body.
For this reason a man shall leave his father and his mother and shall be joined to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct quotation of Gen 2:24 LXX. Paul reads the creation ordinance of marriage typologically as always having pointed toward the union of Christ and the assembly. The "leaving" and "joining" pattern foreshadowed from Eden the mystery of Christ leaving his heavenly Father to be united with his people — the true "one flesh" reality toward which human marriage points (v. 32).

verses.flow: This mystery is great; but I am speaking with reference to Christ and with reference to the assembly.
verses.note: GREEK: μυστήριον (mystērion, G3466) in Pauline usage does not mean "puzzle" but a divine plan once hidden and now revealed (Eph 1:9; 3:3-6; Col 1:26-27). Paul declares that the Gen 2:24 "one flesh" union was always, at its deepest level, about Christ and the assembly. This is the hermeneutical key to the entire passage: human marriage is an icon of the Christ-assembly union, not the reverse.

verses.flow: Nevertheless, let each one of you also so love his own wife as himself, and let the wife see that she fears her husband.
Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right.
"Honor your father and your mother," which is the first commandment with a promise,
verses.note: GREEK: Paul quotes the fifth commandment from Exodus 20:12 / Deuteronomy 5:16 (LXX). The phrase "first commandment with a promise" (entolē prōtē en epangelía) has been debated—it likely means first in prominence among those carrying an explicit blessing, not strictly first in numerical order. TYPOLOGY: The commandment bridges the Decalogue into the new covenant household, showing that Torah's moral imperatives remain operative in Christ.

verses.flow: "that it may be well with you, and you may be long-lived upon the earth."
And fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the discipline and admonition of the Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: Paroргízō (G3949) means "to provoke to anger," a strong term for exasperating. Paideía (G3809) denotes training or discipline, and nouthesía (G3559) denotes admonition or corrective instruction—together they form a comprehensive model of formative and corrective upbringing. THEOLOGICAL: The qualifier "of the Lord" (kyríou) makes Christ the standard and authority behind all parental instruction.

verses.flow: Bondservants, obey your masters according to the flesh, with fear and trembling, in sincerity of your heart, as unto Christ,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Doûloi (G1401) refers to bondservants or slaves, reflecting the Roman household structure where enslaved persons served under a master (kyrios). Paul does not here endorse the institution but transforms the ethical framework within it—service rendered "as unto Christ" dignifies the servant and relativizes the master's authority. GREEK: Haplotēs (G572) connotes singleness of heart, undivided sincerity rather than duplicity.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: not according to eye-service as men-pleasers, but as bondservants of Christ, doing the will of God from the soul,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: with good will rendering service, as to the Lord and not to men,

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing that whatever good each one does, this he shall receive back from the Lord, whether bondservant or free.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And masters, do the same things toward them, giving up threatening, knowing that both their Master and yours is in the heavens, and there is no partiality with him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul strikingly commands masters to "do the same things" (ta auta poieite) toward their bondservants—reciprocity that subverts the Roman social order. The grounding clause—that their Lord and the servants' Lord is one and the same in the heavens—demolishes any basis for partiality. GREEK: Prosōpolēmpsia (G4382) means "respect of persons" or partiality based on external status, a term also used in Romans 2:11 and Colossians 3:25.

verses.flow: Finally, be strengthened in the Lord and in the might of his strength.
Put on the full armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil.
verses.note: GREEK: Panoplía (G3833) denotes the complete suit of armor of a heavy infantryman—every piece, not merely selected parts. Methodeía (G3180) means crafty scheming or strategic deception, used only here and in Ephesians 4:14 in the NT. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The diábolos (G1228, "the slanderer/accuser") stands as the chief adversary whose stratagems are systematic—believers require divine equipment, not human resources, to withstand his cosmic opposition.

verses.flow: For our wrestling is not against blood and flesh, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the world-powers of this darkness, against the spiritual forces of wickedness in the heavenly places.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse catalogs the hostile spiritual hierarchy: archaí (rulers), exousíai (authorities), kosmokrátores (world-rulers) of this present darkness, and pneumatiká tēs ponērías (spiritual forces of wickedness) in the epouránia (heavenly places). These are the rebellious divine council members and their subordinates who exercise illegitimate dominion over the nations (cf. Deut 32:8 LXX; Dan 10:13, 20; Ps 82). GREEK: Kosmokrátōr (G2888) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, attested in astrological and magical texts for powerful cosmic spirits—Paul co-opts the term to identify demonic powers ruling this age's darkness. THEOLOGICAL: The battlefield is explicitly not against human beings (blood and flesh) but against a structured spiritual hierarchy, underscoring that the church's true warfare is transcendent.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: On account of this, take up the full armor of God, that you may be able to resist in the evil day, and having accomplished all things, to stand.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "evil day" (hēmera ponēra) likely refers not to a single eschatological moment alone but to any season of intensified spiritual assault. "Having accomplished all things" (hapanta katergasamenoi) indicates that standing firm requires active engagement—every duty fulfilled—before one can hold the ground. TYPOLOGY: The divine warrior motif echoes Isaiah 59:17 where the Lord himself dons armor; believers now wear the armor that God provides, participating in his own warfare against evil.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Stand therefore, having girded your loins with truth, and having put on the breastplate of righteousness,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and having shod your feet with the readiness of the gospel of peace,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: in all things having taken up the shield of faith, with which you shall be able to quench all the flaming darts of the evil one.
And receive the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God,
verses.note: GREEK: Perikephalaía (G4030) is "helmet," and sōtērion (G4992) means "salvation" as a neuter substantive—echoing Isaiah 59:17 LXX where God puts on the helmet of salvation. Máchaira (G3162) denotes a short sword, and here it is identified as the rhēma theoû—the spoken word of God, distinct from lógos in emphasizing the utterance or declaration. TYPOLOGY: Christ himself wields the sword of his mouth (Rev 19:15); believers participate in the same divine weaponry through Spirit-empowered proclamation of God's word.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: through every prayer and supplication praying at every season in the Spirit, and unto this very thing being watchful in all perseverance and supplication for all the saints,
and on behalf of me, that a word may be given to me in the opening of my mouth, in boldness to make known the mystery of the gospel,
verses.note: GREEK: Ánoixis (G457) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "opening"—Paul requests not merely courage but divinely granted speech itself, that his mouth would be opened with the right words. Mystḗrion (G3466) here refers to the revealed secret of Jew and Gentile united in one body through Christ (cf. Eph 3:3-6). THEOLOGICAL: Even the apostle depends on the prayers of the saints for effective proclamation—apostolic authority does not bypass the need for the Spirit's empowering through intercession.

verses.flow: on behalf of which I am an ambassador in chains, that in it I may speak boldly, as it is necessary for me to speak.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Presbeúō (G4243) means "to serve as an ambassador"—a political term for an official representative of a sovereign. The irony is profound: Paul is an ambassador of the King of all kings, yet he serves en halýsei ("in a chain"), likely referring to his Roman custody. This juxtaposition highlights the paradox of the kingdom of Christ advancing through apparent weakness and suffering.

verses.flow: But that you also may know the things concerning me, how I am doing, Tychicus the beloved brother and faithful servant in the Lord shall make all things known to you,
whom I sent to you for this very purpose, that you may know the things concerning us, and that he may encourage your hearts.
Peace to the brothers, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Grace be with all those who love our Lord Jesus Christ in incorruptibility.
verses.note: GREEK: Aphtharsía (G861) means "incorruptibility" or "imperishability"—the final word of the epistle qualifies the love believers have for the Lord Jesus Christ as undying, unfading, and beyond decay. Some take en aphtharsía with "grace" rather than "love," but the position strongly favors modifying the love of those who love Christ. THEOLOGICAL: The letter that began with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places (1:3) closes with a benediction of grace upon an imperishable love—framing the entire epistle in the eternal, incorruptible purposes of God in Christ.
